en.extract:
The black eagle (Ictinaetus malaiensis) is a bird of prey. Like all eagles, it is in the family Accipitridae, and it is the only member of the genus Ictinaetus. It soars over forests in the hilly regions of tropical and subtropical South and Southeast Asia, as well as southeastern China, hunting mammals and birds, particularly at their nests. It is easily identified by its widely splayed and long primary "fingers", the characteristic silhouette, slow flight and yellow ceres and legs that contrast with the dark feathers.


Taxonomy and systematics
The species name is spelt malayensis in many publications, but the original spelling used by Temminck in his description was malaiensis, according to a 2011 finding of some of the original covers of the part publications, leading to taxonomists applying the principle of priority and rejecting any later spelling emendations.
A molecular phylogenetic study of the Accipitridae published in 2024 found that the black eagle is sister to the three spotted eagles in the genus Clanga.
Two subspecies are recognised:

I. m. perniger (Hodgson, 1836) – north India and Nepal, Bhutan, south India and Sri Lanka.
I. m. malaiensis (Temminck, 1822) – Myanmar, Bangladesh, south China, southeast Asia and the Indonesian Archipelago (except the Lesser Sunda Islands).


Description

The black eagle is a large but slender eagle, at about 75 cm (30 in) in length and 148 to 182 cm (4 ft 10 in to 6 ft 0 in) in wingspan. Despite its large appearance (it is one of the largest eagles in its range), known weights are modest, at between 1,000 and 1,600 g (2.2 and 3.5 lb)—about half the weight of the partially sympatric mountain hawk-eagle, the latter being of a similar total length. Adults have all-black plumage, with a yellow bill base (cere) and feet. The wings are long and pinched in at the innermost flight feathers, giving a distinctive shape. The tail shows faint barring and upper tail covers are paler. On a perched bird, the wing tips reach or exceed the tail tip. The wings are held in a shallow V (wings just above the horizontal plane) in flight. Seen on hot afternoons, scouring the treetops for a nest to maraud, this bird is easily spotted by its black colour, large size, and a characteristic slow flight, sometimes just above the canopy.
Sexes are similar; young birds have a buff head, underparts and underwing coverts. The wing shape helps to distinguish this species from the dark form of changeable hawk-eagle (Nisaetus cirrhatus). The tarsi are fully feathered and the toes are relatively stout and short, with long claws (particularly on the inner toe) that are less strongly curved than in other birds of prey.


Distribution and habitat
The black eagle breeds in tropical and subtropical Asia. Race perniger (Hodgson, 1836) is found in the Himalayan foothills west through Nepal into the Indian states of Himachal Pradesh and Jammu and Kashmir, and in the forests of the Eastern and Western Ghats in peninsular India and Sri Lanka. The bird's westernmost extent is to Gujarat, especially in the forested areas in southern and eastern Gujarat. The species also extends into the Aravalli range of northwestern India. The nominate race malaiensis (Temminck, 1822) is found in Myanmar, Bangladesh, southern China (Yunnan, Fujian) and Taiwan, into Southeast Asia. 
It is generally resident, and no migration has been observed. In a study in southern India, it was found to favour forests with good forest cover and was absent from areas where the cover was less than 50%. Black eagles have been regularly observed in the skies of Bangladesh, where they are known to breed and thrive, particularly in the hilly and forested regions of the country. Their presence is most prominent in the southeastern and northeastern areas, where the terrain is characterized by dense forests and rolling hills. Among these regions, Chittagong and Sylhet stands out as a key habitat for the black eagle.


Behaviour and ecology


Breeding
The courtship display involves steep dives with folded wings with swoops up in a U shape into a vertical stall. They build a platform nest, 3 to 4 feet wide, on a tall tree overlooking a steep valley. One or two white eggs blotched in brown and mauve may be laid during the nesting season between January and April. The nest site may be reused year after year.


Food and feeding
The black eagle eats mammals (including bats, squirrels and other small mammals), birds and eggs. It is a prolific nest-predator and is known for its slow flight just over the canopy. The curved claws and wide gape allow it to pick up eggs of birds from nests, as well as swiftlets from caves. Along with swallow-tailed kites it shares the unique habit of carrying away an entire nest with nestlings to a feeding perch. Squirrels, macaques and many species of birds emit alarm calls when these birds are spotted soaring over the forest. The Indian giant squirrel has been noted as a prey of this species, and young bonnet macaques may also fall prey to them. 


Threats and survival
It is not threatened but is uncommon in large areas in its distribution. Shrinking of forested areas due to large-scale extraction has reduced its earlier range.


Relationship to humans
Due to this eagle's ability to remain aloft for long periods with minimal effort, the Lepcha people of India's Darjeeling district described it as 'the bird that never sat down', while the Soliga people's name (kaana kattale) recalls its black colour and its presence in forested areas.


References


External links

Internet Bird Collection
Notes on the species in Java
Call and sonogram
Video of pair bathing in stream near nesting site


Historical publications
Illustration and description (in French) by Temminck (Pl. Col. vol. 1, plate 117, pages 104–105.)
1836 original description of Nepalese race as Aquila Pernigra by B. H. Hodgson (now a subspecies I. m. perniger).
1843 proposal of new genus Heteropus by Hodgson, separated from Aquila based on the unusual form of the black eagle's foot. With a footnote by Blyth noting that Jerdon had sent specimens to the museum labeled Ictinaëtus ovivorus. Text and illustration.

The '''black eagle''' ('''''Ictinaetus malaiensis''''') is a bird of prey. Like all [[eagle]]s, it is in the family [[Accipitridae]], and it is the only member of the genus '''''Ictinaetus'''''. It soars over [[forest]]s in the hilly regions of tropical and subtropical [[South Asia|South]] and [[Southeast Asia]], as well as southeastern China, hunting mammals and birds, particularly at their [[nest]]s. It is easily identified by its widely splayed and long primary "fingers", the characteristic silhouette, slow flight and yellow [[Cere|ceres]] and legs that contrast with the dark feathers.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The species name is spelt ''malayensis'' in many publications, but the original spelling used by Temminck in his [[Species description|description]] was ''malaiensis'', according to a 2011 finding of some of the original covers of the part publications, leading to taxonomists applying the [[Priority (biology)|principle of priority]] and rejecting any later spelling emendations.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Lebossé, A.|author2=Bour, R.|name-list-style=amp|year=2011|title=The first twenty livraisons of "Les Planches Coloriées d'Oiseaux" of Temminck & Laugier (1820-1839): I. The ten wrappers now known|journal=Zoological Bibliography|volume=1|issue=4|pages=141–150}}</ref>

A [[molecular phylogenetic]] study of the Accipitridae published in 2024 found that the black eagle is [[sister taxon|sister]] to the three spotted eagles in the [[genus]] ''[[Clanga (bird)|Clanga]]''.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Catanach | first1=T.A. | last2=Halley | first2=M.R. | last3=Pirro | first3=S. | date=2024 | title=Enigmas no longer: using ultraconserved elements to place several unusual hawk taxa and address the non-monophyly of the genus ''Accipiter'' (Accipitriformes: Accipitridae) | journal=Biological Journal of the Linnean Society | volume=144 | issue=2 | article-number=blae028 | doi=10.1093/biolinnean/blae028}}</ref>

Two [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=December 2023 | title=Hoatzin, New World vultures, Secretarybird, raptors | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.1  | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/raptors/| publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=6 April 2024 }}</ref>
* ''I. m. perniger'' ([[Brian Houghton Hodgson|Hodgson]], 1836) – north India and Nepal, Bhutan, south India and Sri Lanka.<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |title=World Species: Ictinaetus malayensis (Black Eagle) |url=https://worldspecies.org/ntaxa/829742 |access-date=2024-10-15 |website=worldspecies.org}}</ref>
* ''I. m. malaiensis'' ([[Coenraad Jacob Temminck|Temminck]], 1822) – Myanmar, Bangladesh, south China, southeast Asia and the [[List of islands of Indonesia|Indonesian Archipelago]] (except the [[Lesser Sunda Islands]]).<ref name=":1" />

==Description==
[[File:Black Eagle wings.jpg|thumb| left | Black eagle wing span]]
The black eagle is a large but slender eagle, at about {{convert|75|cm|inch|abbr=on}} in length and {{convert|148|to|182|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}} in [[wingspan]]. Despite its large appearance (it is one of the largest eagles in its range), known weights are modest, at between {{convert|1000|and|1600|g|lb|abbr=on}}—about half the weight of the partially [[Sympatry|sympatric]] [[mountain hawk-eagle]], the latter being of a similar total length. Adults have all-black plumage, with a yellow bill base (cere) and feet. The wings are long and pinched in at the innermost flight feathers, giving a distinctive shape. The tail shows faint barring and upper tail covers are paler. On a perched bird, the wing tips reach or exceed the tail tip. The wings are held in a shallow V (wings just above the horizontal plane) in flight. Seen on hot afternoons, scouring the treetops for a nest to maraud, this bird is easily spotted by its black colour, large size, and a characteristic slow flight, sometimes just above the canopy.<ref name=ras>{{cite book|author1=Rasmussen, P.C.  |author2=J.C. Anderton  |name-list-style=amp|year=2005|title= Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide. Vol. 2.|publisher=Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions|page=104}}</ref>

Sexes are similar; young birds have a buff head, underparts and underwing coverts. The wing shape helps to distinguish this species from the dark form of [[changeable hawk-eagle]] (''Nisaetus cirrhatus''). The tarsi are fully feathered and the toes are relatively stout and short, with long claws (particularly on the inner toe<ref>{{cite journal|title=Description of a new genus of Falconidae|author=Hodgson, B.H.|year=1843|pages=127–128|url= https://archive.org/stream/journalofasiatic121asia#page/127/mode/1up|journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal}}</ref>) that are less strongly curved than in other birds of prey.<ref name=hbk>{{cite book|author1=Ali S  |author2=SD Ripley |name-list-style=amp| pages=283–285| title=Handbook of the birds of India and Pakistan|publisher=Oxford University Press| volume=1 |edition=2nd| year=1978}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The black eagle breeds in tropical and subtropical [[Asia]]. Race ''perniger'' (Hodgson, 1836) is found in the [[Himalaya]]n foothills west through Nepal into the Indian states of [[Himachal Pradesh]] and [[Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)|Jammu and Kashmir]], and in the forests of the Eastern and [[Western Ghats]] in peninsular [[India]] and [[Sri Lanka]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Prater, S.H.|year=1940|title=The Indian Black Eagle (''Ictinaetus malayanus perniger'' Hodgs.) in Salsette|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47819479|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=41|issue=4|page=899}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Rao, VUS|year=1968|title=The Black Eagle ''Ictinaetus malayensis perniger'' within Bombay limits|journal=[[Newsletter for Birdwatchers]]|volume=8|issue=12|pages=6–7}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Burgess, H.E.|year=1937|title=Eagles on the Nilgiris|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47602147|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=39|issue=2|pages=399–403}}</ref> The bird's westernmost extent is to [[Gujarat]], especially in the forested areas in southern and eastern Gujarat.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Parasharya|first=B. M.|date=27 February 2010|title=Black Eagle Ictinaetus malayensis at Narmada Dam, Gujarat|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/302927984|journal=Indian Birds|volume=6|pages=95–96|via=Research Gate}}</ref> The species also extends into the Aravalli range of northwestern India.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Dharmakumarsinhji, KS|author-link=Raol Shri Dharmakumarsinhji|year=1985|title=The Black Eagle ''Ictinaetus malayensis'' Temm. at Sawai Madhopur, (Rajasthan)|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/50395355|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=82|issue=3|page=655}}</ref> The nominate race ''malaiensis'' (Temminck, 1822) is found in Myanmar, Bangladesh, southern China (Yunnan, Fujian) and Taiwan, into Southeast Asia. 

It is generally resident, and no migration has been observed.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Tordoff, A.W.|year=2002|title=Raptor migration at Hoang Lien Nature Reserve, northern Vietnam|url=http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/18pdfs/Tordoff-Raptor.pdf|journal=Forktail|volume=18|pages=45–48|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110610174902/http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/18pdfs/Tordoff-Raptor.pdf|archive-date=2011-06-10}}</ref><ref name=":1" /> In a study in southern India, it was found to favour forests with good forest cover and was absent from areas where the cover was less than 50%.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Thiollay, Jean-Marc|year=1993|title=Response of a Raptor Community to Shrinking Area and Degradation of Tropical Rain Forest in the South Western Ghats (India)|journal=Ecography|volume=16|issue=2|pages=97–110|doi=10.1111/j.1600-0587.1993.tb00062.x|bibcode=1993Ecogr..16...97T }}</ref> Black eagles have been regularly observed in the skies of [[Bangladesh]], where they are known to breed and thrive, particularly in the hilly and forested regions of the country. Their presence is most prominent in the southeastern and northeastern areas, where the terrain is characterized by dense forests and rolling hills. Among these regions, [[Chittagong Division|Chittagong]] and [[Sylhet Division|Sylhet]] stands out as a key habitat for the black eagle.<ref>{{Cite web |last=admin-zaber |date=2023-03-05 |title=বাংলাদেশের দিবাচর শিকারী পাখি |url=https://www.birdingbd.com/2023/03/05/diurnal-raptors-bangladesh/ |access-date=2024-10-13 |website=Birding in Bangladesh |language=en-US}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2016 |title=Black Eagle Ictinaetus malaiensis has most recently been assessed for The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species in 2016. Ictinaetus malaiensis is listed as Least Concern. |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/22696019/93538909}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Rahman |first=Aminur |date=2021 |title=বিরল বড় কালো ঈগল |url=https://samakal.com/bangladesh/article/83885/%E0%A6%AC%E0%A6%BF%E0%A6%B0%E0%A6%B2-%E0%A6%AC%E0%A7%9C-%E0%A6%95%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%B2%E0%A7%8B-%E0%A6%88%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%B2}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Islam |first=Muhammad Anwarul |date=2015 |title=ঈগল |url=https://bn.banglapedia.org/index.php?title=%E0%A6%88%E0%A6%97%E0%A6%B2}}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
===Breeding===
The courtship display involves steep dives with folded wings with swoops up in a U shape into a vertical stall.<ref name="ras" /> They build a platform nest, 3 to 4 feet wide, on a tall tree overlooking a steep valley. One or two white eggs blotched in brown and mauve may be laid during the nesting season between January and April.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Buchanan, Kenneth|year=1899|title=Nesting of the Black Eagle|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7148525|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=12|issue=4|pages=776–777}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Daly, W Mahon|year=1899|title=Nesting of the Black Eagle|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7148314|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=12|issue=3|page=589}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Baker, ECS|year=1918|title=Notes on the nidification of some Indian Falconidae. III. the genera ''Ictinaetus'' and ''Microhierax''|journal=Ibis|volume=60|issue=1|pages=51–68|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1918.tb00770.x|url=https://zenodo.org/record/2090205}}[https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/55021#page/101/ BHL]</ref> The nest site may be reused year after year.<ref name="hbk" />
<gallery mode="packed">
File:Black eagle 9.jpg|Black eagle nest
File:Black eagle 6.jpg|Black eagle bringing materials for nesting.
File:Black Eagle in Kaggalipura.JPG|The primaries are long and well splayed in slow flight
</gallery>

===Food and feeding===
The black eagle eats [[mammal]]s (including bats,<ref name=":0" /> squirrels and other small mammals), [[bird]]s and eggs. It is a prolific nest-predator and is known for its slow flight just over the canopy.<ref name="ras" /> The curved claws and wide gape allow it to pick up eggs of birds from nests, as well as swiftlets from caves.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1080/08927014.2004.9522651|author=Nijman, V|year=2004|title=Seasonal variation in naturally occurring mobbing behavior of drongos (Dicruridae) towards two avian predators|journal=Ethology Ecology & Evolution|volume=16|issue=1 |pages=25–32|bibcode=2004EtEcE..16...25N |url=http://ejour-fup.unifi.it/index.php/eee/article/view/981/927|format=PDF|access-date=2009-04-10|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111001015852/http://ejour-fup.unifi.it/index.php/eee/article/view/981/927|archive-date=2011-10-01|url-access=subscription}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book|title=The Birds of Borneo (2nd edn).|last=Smythies|first=Bertram|publisher=Oliver and Boyd: Edinburgh.|year=1968|isbn=978-0-05-001530-8}}</ref> Along with [[swallow-tailed kite]]s it shares the unique habit of carrying away an entire nest with nestlings to a feeding perch.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Coulson, Jennifer O.|year=2001|title= Swallow-tailed Kites Carry Passerine Nests Containing Nestlings to Their Own Nests|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=113|issue=3|pages=340–342|doi=10.1676/0043-5643(2001)113[0340:STKCPN]2.0.CO;2}}</ref> Squirrels, macaques and many species of birds emit alarm calls when these birds are spotted soaring over the forest. The [[Indian giant squirrel]] has been noted as a prey of this species<ref>{{cite journal|author=Borges, Renee |year=1986|title=Predation attempt by Black Eagle (''Ictinaetus malayensis perniger'') on Indian Giant Squirrel (''Ratufa indica elphinstonii'')|journal= J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=83|page=203|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48772393}}</ref>, and young [[bonnet macaque]]s may also fall prey to them.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Ali, Rauf|year=1986|title=Feeding ecology of the Bonnet Macaque at the Mundanthurai Sanctuary, Tamil Nadu|journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc.|volume=83|issue=1|pages=98–110|url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48761050}}</ref> 

===Threats and survival===
It is not threatened but is uncommon in large areas in its distribution. Shrinking of forested areas due to large-scale extraction has reduced its earlier range.

==Relationship to humans==
Due to this eagle's ability to remain aloft for long periods with minimal effort, the [[Lepcha people]] of India's [[Darjeeling district]] described it as 'the bird that never sat down', while the [[Soliga people]]'s name (''kaana kattale)'' recalls its black colour and its presence in forested areas.<ref>{{cite book|title=A Monograph of the Birds of Prey (Order Accipitres), Part XI.|author=Swann, H. Kirke|publisher=Wheldon & Wesley, London|year=1924–1945|editor=Alexander Wetmore|page=93}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=http://jlrexplore.com/explore/on-assignment/soligas-people-of-the-forest|title=Soligas: the people of the forest|last=Srinivas|first=Prashanth|website=JLRExplore|date=January 2014 |access-date=23 December 2018}}</ref>

* 1836 original [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/114404#page/275/ description] of Nepalese race as ''Aquila Pernigra'' by [[Brian Houghton Hodgson|B. H. Hodgson]] (now a subspecies ''I. m. perniger''). 
* 1843 [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/123801#page/163/ proposal of new genus] ''Heteropus'' by Hodgson, separated from ''Aquila'' based on the unusual form of the black eagle's foot. With a footnote by Blyth noting that [[Thomas C. Jerdon|Jerdon]] had sent specimens to the museum labeled ''Ictinaëtus ovivorus''. Text and illustration.

zh.extract:
林鵰（学名：Ictinaetus malayensis）为鹰科林鵰属的鸟类，分布于中華人民共和國、台灣、东南亚和南亚的巴基斯坦、印度和斯里兰卡等地区。常见于山林以及筑巢于常绿森林的高树上。该物种的模式产地在印度尼西亚爪哇。


特徵

為大型猛禽，身長約67至81cm，翅膀伸展後長約164至178cm，是臺灣留鳥猛禽中翼展最長的。
雌雄鳥同型，全身大致上為暗褐色，尾羽約有8道不明顯的淡色橫紋。
嘴基為黃色，先端灰色，蠟膜黃色；眼暗褐色，眼先灰色，
眉骨突出；毛足，趾黃色，爪黑色，爪彎曲度甚小，爪形由前看由外趾、中趾、而內趾逐漸加長，
此為辨識上的重點。飛行時，雙翼寬長、近體側較窄，翼端指叉非常深，尾羽成角型。


習性
林鵰偏好較原始、樹木較多的山區。在中國大陸和台灣，通常只有在高山才見得到。牠們常在天氣晴朗的時候，在山區中央的上空盤旋鳴叫，並伺機尋找飛鼠、松鼠等嚙齒類動物，有時也會捕捉麂或獼猴的幼獸。
棲地分佈：
分佈於印度、斯里蘭卡、中南半島、馬來半島、蘇門答臘、爪哇、峇里島、婆羅洲、蘇拉威西、摩鹿加群島、華南及臺灣。
在臺灣為稀有留鳥，廣泛分佈於臺灣本島海拔2,600公尺以下的山地森林，北部與東部略多於南部，
墾丁國家公園於秋季偶有發現紀錄，陽明山國家公園曾於2005年首度發現2隻之後，這十多年來已經常被記錄到，離島無分佈。


保护
中国国家重点保护野生动物等级：二级
中華民國農業部林業保育署公告保育類野生動物等级：一级


亚种
林鵰指名亚种（学名：Ictinaetus malaiensis malaiensis）：分布于台湾中海拔山區、中國南方（秦嶺南部、四川、雲南與多數東南沿海省分）、中南半島至印尼除小巽他群島外的多數島嶼。该物种的模式产地在印度尼西亚爪哇。
林鵰南亞亞種（学名：Ictinaetus malaiensis perniger）：分布於印度與斯里蘭卡。
